title: Population and Lndscape Change in teh Denver Metro Area

yaml.title: Population and Lndscape Change in teh Denver Metro Area

markdown.markdown:
```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(tidycensus)
library(tidyverse)
library(tigris)
library(sf)
library(tmap)
library(scales)

census_api_key("9383d03114a6fd61b90f6aa740342df389ec984f",
               install = TRUE, overwrite = TRUE)
options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)

# REPLICATION STUDY — Denver Metro Area
# Author: Linda Adade
# Date: 05/08/2026
# Hanberry (2022) Landscape Classification
# Walker (2023) Analyzing US Census Data
# ============================================================

# LIBRARIES
library(tidycensus)
library(tidyverse)
library(tigris)
library(sf)
library(tmap)
library(scales)

census_api_key("9383d03114a6fd61b90f6aa740342df389ec984f",
               install   = TRUE,
               overwrite = TRUE)

options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)

# ============================================================
# STEP 1 — Download 2020 Census tracts for Colorado
# Walker Ch. 2.1.1 — get_decennial(), sumfile = "pl" for 2020
# ============================================================
co_tracts <- get_decennial(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = "P1_001N",
  state     = "CO",
  geometry  = TRUE,
  year      = 2020,
  sumfile   = "pl"
)

# ============================================================
# STEP 2 — Download Denver MSA boundary
# Walker Ch. 7.1.2 — core_based_statistical_areas()
# ============================================================
denver_msa <- core_based_statistical_areas(cb = TRUE, year = 2020) %>%
  filter(str_detect(NAME, "Denver-Aurora")) %>%
  st_transform(26954)

# ============================================================
# STEP 3 — Spatially filter tracts to Denver MSA
# Walker Ch. 7.1.2 — st_filter() with st_within predicate
# CRS 26954 = NAD83 Colorado Central (meters) for area calc
# ============================================================
co_tracts     <- co_tracts %>% st_transform(26954)
denver_tracts <- co_tracts %>%
  st_filter(denver_msa, .predicate = st_within)

# ============================================================
# STEP 4 — Calculate population density and classify landscapes
# Walker Ch. 3.3.2 — mutate() and case_when()
# Hanberry (2022) Table 4 Worldpop thresholds (p.8)
# Units: persons per square kilometer
# ============================================================
denver_density <- denver_tracts %>%
  rename(pop_total = value) %>%
  mutate(
    area_sqmi   = as.numeric(st_area(geometry)) / 2589988,
    area_km2    = area_sqmi / 0.386,
    pop_density = pop_total / area_km2,
    landscape   = case_when(
      is.na(pop_density)  ~ "Exurban",
      pop_density >  1900 ~ "Urban High-Density",
      pop_density >=  800 ~ "Urban Low-Density",
      pop_density >=  550 ~ "Suburban High-Density",
      pop_density >=  250 ~ "Suburban Low-Density",
      TRUE                ~ "Exurban"
    ),
    landscape = factor(landscape, levels = c(
      "Urban High-Density",
      "Urban Low-Density",
      "Suburban High-Density",
      "Suburban Low-Density",
      "Exurban"
    ))
  )

# Confirm classification
table(denver_density$landscape)
summary(denver_density$pop_density)

# ============================================================
# STEP 5 — Create plot versions (WGS84 for ggplot mapping)
# ============================================================
denver_density_plot <- denver_density %>% st_transform(4326)
denver_msa_plot     <- denver_msa     %>% st_transform(4326)

# ============================================================
# STEP 6 — Landscape classification map
# Walker Ch. 6 — ggplot2 geom_sf()
# ============================================================
landscape_map <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data  = denver_density_plot,
          aes(fill = landscape),
          color = "white", lwd = 0.1) +
  geom_sf(data  = denver_msa_plot,
          fill  = NA, color = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Urban High-Density"    = "#b2182b",
      "Urban Low-Density"     = "#ef8a62",
      "Suburban High-Density" = "#9ecae1",
      "Suburban Low-Density"  = "#d1e5f0",
      "Exurban"               = "#2166ac"
    ),
    name = "Hanberry Landscape\n(Table 4 Worldpop)"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Denver Metro Area: Urban–Suburban–Exurban\nLandscape (2020)",
    subtitle = "Classified using Hanberry (2022) Worldpop thresholds",
    caption  = "Source: 2020 Decennial Census · Hanberry (2022) Table 4"
  ) +
  theme_void(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13,
                                   margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey40",
                                   margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.caption    = element_text(size = 8,  color = "grey50"),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title    = element_text(size = 9,  face = "bold"),
    legend.text     = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.margin     = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

landscape_map
# ============================================================
# STEP 7 — Download ACS demographic variables
# Walker Ch. 2.1.2 — get_acs(), survey = "acs5"
# Walker Ch. 3 — variable codes explained p.27-34
# ============================================================
denver_acs <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = c(
    total_pop    = "B02001_001",  # race universe
    white        = "B02001_002",  # White alone
    black        = "B02001_003",  # Black alone
    hispanic     = "B03003_003",  # Hispanic/Latino (ethnicity)
    med_income   = "B19013_001",  # median HH income
    below_pov    = "B17001_002",  # below poverty line
    pov_universe = "B17001_001",  # poverty universe
    bach         = "B15003_022",  # bachelor's degree
    masters      = "B15003_023",  # master's degree
    professional = "B15003_024",  # professional degree
    doctorate    = "B15003_025",  # doctorate degree
    edu_universe = "B15003_001"   # education universe (25+)
  ),
  state  = "CO",
  year   = 2020,
  survey = "acs5"
)

# ============================================================
# STEP 8 — Reshape and join ACS to spatial data
# Walker Ch. 3.2 — pivot_wider() and left_join()
# Walker p.34 — Hispanic is ethnicity not race (separate table)
# ============================================================
denver_acs_wide <- denver_acs %>%
  select(GEOID, variable, estimate) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = variable, values_from = estimate)

denver_full <- denver_density %>%
  left_join(denver_acs_wide, by = "GEOID") %>%
  mutate(
    pct_white   = white    / total_pop   * 100,
    pct_black   = black    / total_pop   * 100,
    pct_hisp    = hispanic / total_pop   * 100,
    pct_poverty = below_pov / pov_universe * 100,
    pct_college = (bach + masters + professional + doctorate) /
                   edu_universe * 100
  )

denver_full_plot <- denver_full %>% st_transform(4326)

# ============================================================
# STEP 9 — Summary table by landscape
# Walker Ch. 3.3.1 — group_by() and summarize()
# ============================================================
landscape_summary <- denver_full %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(landscape) %>%
  summarize(
    total_pop   = sum(pop_total,   na.rm = TRUE),
    med_income  = median(med_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_white   = weighted.mean(pct_white,   pop_total,    na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_black   = weighted.mean(pct_black,   pop_total,    na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_hisp    = weighted.mean(pct_hisp,    pop_total,    na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_poverty = weighted.mean(pct_poverty, pov_universe, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_college = weighted.mean(pct_college, edu_universe, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

print(landscape_summary, width = Inf)

# ============================================================
# STEP 10 — Income bar chart
# Walker Ch. 4.1 — ggplot2 geom_col()
# Walker Ch. 4.2.3 — ggsave()
# ============================================================
income_chart <- ggplot(
  landscape_summary,
  aes(x = landscape, y = med_income, fill = landscape)
) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = dollar(med_income)), vjust = -0.4, size = 3.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = dollar_format(),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Urban High-Density"    = "#b2182b",
    "Urban Low-Density"     = "#ef8a62",
    "Suburban High-Density" = "#9ecae1",
    "Suburban Low-Density"  = "#d1e5f0",
    "Exurban"               = "#2166ac"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Median Household Income by Landscape — Denver MSA",
    subtitle = "2016–2020 ACS 5-Year Estimates",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Median Household Income",
    caption  = "Source: U.S. Census Bureau ACS"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))

income_chart

# ============================================================
# STEP 11 — Poverty rate choropleth map
# Walker Ch. 6 — tmap/ggplot2 choropleth mapping
# ============================================================
denver_poverty_plot <- denver_full %>%
  st_transform(4326) %>%
  select(GEOID, pct_poverty, geometry) %>%
  filter(!is.na(pct_poverty)) %>%
  mutate(
    poverty_cat = cut(
      pct_poverty,
      breaks = c(0, 2.745, 5.189, 8.247, 12.802, 77.935),
      labels = c(
        "Very Low (0–2.7%)",
        "Low (2.7–5.2%)",
        "Moderate (5.2–8.2%)",
        "High (8.2–12.8%)",
        "Very High (12.8–77.9%)"
      ),
      include.lowest = TRUE
    )
  )

poverty_map <- ggplot(denver_poverty_plot) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = poverty_cat), color = "white", lwd = 0.1) +
  geom_sf(data = denver_msa_plot, fill = NA,
          color = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Very Low (0–2.7%)"      = "#fee5d9",
      "Low (2.7–5.2%)"         = "#fcae91",
      "Moderate (5.2–8.2%)"    = "#fb6a4a",
      "High (8.2–12.8%)"       = "#cb181d",
      "Very High (12.8–77.9%)" = "#67000d"
    ),
    name     = "% Below Poverty Line",
    na.value = "grey80"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Poverty Rate by Tract —\nDenver MSA (2020 ACS)",
    subtitle = "Quantile classification · 2016–2020 ACS 5-Year Estimates",
    caption  = "Source: U.S. Census Bureau ACS"
  ) +
  theme_void(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13,
                                   margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey40",
                                   margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.caption    = element_text(size = 8,  color = "grey50"),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title    = element_text(size = 9,  face = "bold"),
    legend.text     = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.margin     = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

poverty_map

# ============================================================
# STEP 12 — Racial composition bar chart
# Walker Ch. 4 — ggplot2 grouped bar chart
# Walker p.34 — Hispanic as separate ethnic category
# ============================================================
race_summary <- denver_full %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(landscape) %>%
  summarize(
    White    = weighted.mean(pct_white, pop_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    Black    = weighted.mean(pct_black, pop_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    Hispanic = weighted.mean(pct_hisp,  pop_total, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = c(White, Black, Hispanic),
    names_to  = "group",
    values_to = "pct"
  ) %>%
  mutate(group = factor(group, levels = c("White", "Hispanic", "Black")))

race_chart <- ggplot(
  race_summary,
  aes(x = landscape, y = pct, fill = group)
) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.7),
    vjust    = -0.4,
    size     = 2.8
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "White"    = "#2166ac",
      "Black"    = "#b2182b",
      "Hispanic" = "#f4a582"
    ),
    name = "Racial group"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0(round(x), "%"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Racial Composition by Landscape — Denver MSA",
    subtitle = "Population-weighted averages · 2016–2020 ACS 5-Year Estimates",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Share of population (%)",
    caption  = "Source: U.S. Census Bureau ACS"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13,
                                    margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey40",
                                    margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.caption     = element_text(size = 8,  color = "grey50"),
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1),
    legend.position  = "right",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

race_chart

# ============================================================
# STEP 13 — Hispanic population share map
# Walker Ch. 6 — choropleth mapping
# ============================================================
denver_hisp_plot <- denver_full %>%
  st_transform(4326) %>%
  select(GEOID, pct_hisp, geometry) %>%
  filter(!is.na(pct_hisp)) %>%
  mutate(
    hisp_cat = cut(
      pct_hisp,
      breaks = c(0, 10, 20, 35, 50, 100),
      labels = c(
        "Very Low (0–10%)",
        "Low (10–20%)",
        "Moderate (20–35%)",
        "High (35–50%)",
        "Very High (50–100%)"
      ),
      include.lowest = TRUE
    )
  )

hisp_map <- ggplot(denver_hisp_plot) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = hisp_cat), color = "white", lwd = 0.1) +
  geom_sf(data = denver_msa_plot, fill = NA,
          color = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Very Low (0–10%)"    = "#f7f4f9",
      "Low (10–20%)"        = "#d4b9da",
      "Moderate (20–35%)"   = "#c994c7",
      "High (35–50%)"       = "#df65b0",
      "Very High (50–100%)" = "#91003f"
    ),
    name     = "% Hispanic",
    na.value = "grey80"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Hispanic Population Share by Tract —\nDenver MSA",
    subtitle = "2016–2020 ACS 5-Year Estimates",
    caption  = "Source: U.S. Census Bureau ACS"
  ) +
  theme_void(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13,
                                   margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey40",
                                   margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.caption    = element_text(size = 8,  color = "grey50"),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title    = element_text(size = 9,  face = "bold"),
    legend.text     = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.margin     = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

hisp_map

# ============================================================
# STEP 14 — Population pyramids
# Walker Ch. 4.5 p.79-82 — population pyramids
# B01001 = Sex by Age table
# Males flipped negative per Walker p.81
# ============================================================
male_vars_list   <- paste0("B01001_0", str_pad(3:25,  2, pad = "0"))
female_vars_list <- paste0("B01001_0", str_pad(27:49, 2, pad = "0"))

age_sex <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = c(male_vars_list, female_vars_list),
  state     = "CO",
  year      = 2020,
  survey    = "acs5"
)

male_vars   <- paste0("B01001_0", str_pad(3:25,  2, pad = "0"))
female_vars <- paste0("B01001_0", str_pad(27:49, 2, pad = "0"))

age_labels <- c(
  "Under 5", "5–9", "10–14", "15–17", "18–19",
  "20", "21", "22–24", "25–29", "30–34",
  "35–39", "40–44", "45–49", "50–54", "55–59",
  "60–61", "62–64", "65–66", "67–69", "70–74",
  "75–79", "80–84", "85+"
)

urban_ids <- denver_density %>%
  filter(str_detect(landscape, "Urban")) %>%
  pull(GEOID)

suburban_ids <- denver_density %>%
  filter(str_detect(landscape, "Suburban")) %>%
  pull(GEOID)

exurban_ids <- denver_density %>%
  filter(landscape == "Exurban") %>%
  pull(GEOID)

make_pyramid_data <- function(geoids) {
  age_sex %>%
    filter(GEOID %in% geoids) %>%
    mutate(sex = case_when(
      variable %in% male_vars   ~ "Male",
      variable %in% female_vars ~ "Female",
      TRUE                      ~ NA_character_
    )) %>%
    filter(!is.na(sex)) %>%
    group_by(variable, sex) %>%
    summarize(pop = sum(estimate, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
    group_by(sex) %>%
    mutate(age_group = age_labels) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    mutate(
      age_group = factor(age_group, levels = age_labels),
      pop       = if_else(sex == "Male", -pop, pop)
    )
}

make_pyramid_plot <- function(data, title_text) {
  max_val <- max(abs(data$pop), na.rm = TRUE)
  ggplot(data, aes(x = pop, y = age_group, fill = sex)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.85, alpha = 0.85) +
    scale_x_continuous(
      labels = ~ scales::number(abs(.x),
                                scale    = 1e-3,
                                suffix   = "k",
                                accuracy = 1),
      limits = c(-max_val, max_val),
      breaks = pretty(c(-max_val, max_val), n = 6)
    ) +
    scale_fill_manual(
      values = c(Female = "#b2182b", Male = "#2166ac")
    ) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "white", lwd = 0.8) +
    labs(
      title    = title_text,
      subtitle = "Denver MSA · 2016–2020 ACS",
      x        = "Population",
      y        = "Age Group",
      fill     = "",
      caption  = "Source: U.S. Census Bureau ACS B01001"
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(
      plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13,
                                      margin = margin(b = 4)),
      plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey40",
                                      margin = margin(b = 8)),
      plot.caption     = element_text(size = 8,  color = "grey50"),
      legend.position  = "bottom",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    )
}

urban_pyramid_data    <- make_pyramid_data(urban_ids)
suburban_pyramid_data <- make_pyramid_data(suburban_ids)
exurban_pyramid_data  <- make_pyramid_data(exurban_ids)

pyramid_urban <- make_pyramid_plot(
  urban_pyramid_data,
  "Population Pyramid — Urban Tracts\nDenver MSA"
)

pyramid_suburban <- make_pyramid_plot(
  suburban_pyramid_data,
  "Population Pyramid — Suburban Tracts\nDenver MSA"
)

pyramid_exurban <- make_pyramid_plot(
  exurban_pyramid_data,
  "Population Pyramid — Exurban Tracts\nDenver MSA"
)

pyramid_urban
pyramid_suburban
pyramid_exurban



# ============================================================
# STEP 15 — Confirm all output files saved
# ============================================================
list.files(pattern = "\\.png$")
# (keep all your existing code here, just no ggsave lines needed)
```

## Overview

This study examines demographic change and spatial inequality across the Denver-Aurora Metropolitan Statistical Area. Using data from the 2020 Decennial Census and the 2016-2020 American Community Survey (ACS), census tracts are classified into urban, suburban, and exurban landscape types based on population density. This classification is then used to examine how income, poverty, racial composition, and age structure vary across this landscape, addressing the question: how do these dimensions of inequality differ across the urban–suburban–exurban continuum, and what do these patterns reveal about metropolitan growth in Denver?
Census tracts are classified using population density thresholds adapted from Hanberry (2022), which distinguishes urban, suburban, and exurban landscapes based on persons per square kilometer. This framework provides a systematic basis for comparing demographic patterns across landscape types and allows for the analysis of spatial variation that may be obscured when relying solely on administrative boundaries.




## Landscape Classification

Urban high-density tracts concentrate in the city core while suburban 
and exurban tracts extend toward the periphery.

```{r}
landscape_map
```

## Income

Median household income rises moving from urban to suburban and exurban 
landscapes, reflecting uneven distribution of economic gains across the metro.

```{r}
income_chart
```

## Poverty

Poverty concentrates heavily in urban core tracts, particularly in 
north and west Denver.

```{r}
poverty_map
```

## Racial Composition

Hispanic residents concentrate in urban and inner suburban tracts while 
suburban and exurban landscapes remain predominantly white.

```{r}
race_chart
hisp_map
```

## Age Structure

Urban tracts skew younger, suburban tracts reflect family households, 
and exurban tracts show an older age profile.

```{r}
pyramid_urban
pyramid_suburban
pyramid_exurban
```

## What the Data Reveal

Taken together, these patterns tell a consistent story about uneven 
development in the Denver metro area. Suburban and exurban tracts 
concentrate wealth, older family households, and predominantly white 
populations, while urban core tracts bear the highest poverty rates 
and are home to the metro's Hispanic and Black residents. The density 
gradient is not simply a technical classification — it maps onto deeper 
inequalities of race and income that shape who benefits from Denver's 
growth and who remains on the margins.
markdown.srcMarkdownNoYaml:
```{r setup, include=FALSE}
library(tidycensus)
library(tidyverse)
library(tigris)
library(sf)
library(tmap)
library(scales)

census_api_key("9383d03114a6fd61b90f6aa740342df389ec984f",
               install = TRUE, overwrite = TRUE)
options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)

# # ============================================================
# REPLICATION STUDY — Denver Metro Area
# Author: Linda Adade
# Date: 05/08/2026
# Hanberry (2022) Landscape Classification
# Walker (2023) Analyzing US Census Data
# ============================================================

# LIBRARIES
library(tidycensus)
library(tidyverse)
library(tigris)
library(sf)
library(tmap)
library(scales)

census_api_key("9383d03114a6fd61b90f6aa740342df389ec984f",
               install   = TRUE,
               overwrite = TRUE)

options(tigris_use_cache = TRUE)

# ============================================================
# STEP 1 — Download 2020 Census tracts for Colorado
# Walker Ch. 2.1.1 — get_decennial(), sumfile = "pl" for 2020
# ============================================================
co_tracts <- get_decennial(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = "P1_001N",
  state     = "CO",
  geometry  = TRUE,
  year      = 2020,
  sumfile   = "pl"
)

# ============================================================
# STEP 2 — Download Denver MSA boundary
# Walker Ch. 7.1.2 — core_based_statistical_areas()
# ============================================================
denver_msa <- core_based_statistical_areas(cb = TRUE, year = 2020) %>%
  filter(str_detect(NAME, "Denver-Aurora")) %>%
  st_transform(26954)

# ============================================================
# STEP 3 — Spatially filter tracts to Denver MSA
# Walker Ch. 7.1.2 — st_filter() with st_within predicate
# CRS 26954 = NAD83 Colorado Central (meters) for area calc
# ============================================================
co_tracts     <- co_tracts %>% st_transform(26954)
denver_tracts <- co_tracts %>%
  st_filter(denver_msa, .predicate = st_within)

# ============================================================
# STEP 4 — Calculate population density and classify landscapes
# Walker Ch. 3.3.2 — mutate() and case_when()
# Hanberry (2022) Table 4 Worldpop thresholds (p.8)
# Units: persons per square kilometer
# ============================================================
denver_density <- denver_tracts %>%
  rename(pop_total = value) %>%
  mutate(
    area_sqmi   = as.numeric(st_area(geometry)) / 2589988,
    area_km2    = area_sqmi / 0.386,
    pop_density = pop_total / area_km2,
    landscape   = case_when(
      is.na(pop_density)  ~ "Exurban",
      pop_density >  1900 ~ "Urban High-Density",
      pop_density >=  800 ~ "Urban Low-Density",
      pop_density >=  550 ~ "Suburban High-Density",
      pop_density >=  250 ~ "Suburban Low-Density",
      TRUE                ~ "Exurban"
    ),
    landscape = factor(landscape, levels = c(
      "Urban High-Density",
      "Urban Low-Density",
      "Suburban High-Density",
      "Suburban Low-Density",
      "Exurban"
    ))
  )

# Confirm classification
table(denver_density$landscape)
summary(denver_density$pop_density)

# ============================================================
# STEP 5 — Create plot versions (WGS84 for ggplot mapping)
# ============================================================
denver_density_plot <- denver_density %>% st_transform(4326)
denver_msa_plot     <- denver_msa     %>% st_transform(4326)

# ============================================================
# STEP 6 — Landscape classification map
# Walker Ch. 6 — ggplot2 geom_sf()
# ============================================================
landscape_map <- ggplot() +
  geom_sf(data  = denver_density_plot,
          aes(fill = landscape),
          color = "white", lwd = 0.1) +
  geom_sf(data  = denver_msa_plot,
          fill  = NA, color = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Urban High-Density"    = "#b2182b",
      "Urban Low-Density"     = "#ef8a62",
      "Suburban High-Density" = "#9ecae1",
      "Suburban Low-Density"  = "#d1e5f0",
      "Exurban"               = "#2166ac"
    ),
    name = "Hanberry Landscape\n(Table 4 Worldpop)"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Denver Metro Area: Urban–Suburban–Exurban\nLandscape (2020)",
    subtitle = "Classified using Hanberry (2022) Worldpop thresholds",
    caption  = "Source: 2020 Decennial Census · Hanberry (2022) Table 4"
  ) +
  theme_void(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13,
                                   margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey40",
                                   margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.caption    = element_text(size = 8,  color = "grey50"),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title    = element_text(size = 9,  face = "bold"),
    legend.text     = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.margin     = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

landscape_map
# ============================================================
# STEP 7 — Download ACS demographic variables
# Walker Ch. 2.1.2 — get_acs(), survey = "acs5"
# Walker Ch. 3 — variable codes explained p.27-34
# ============================================================
denver_acs <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = c(
    total_pop    = "B02001_001",  # race universe
    white        = "B02001_002",  # White alone
    black        = "B02001_003",  # Black alone
    hispanic     = "B03003_003",  # Hispanic/Latino (ethnicity)
    med_income   = "B19013_001",  # median HH income
    below_pov    = "B17001_002",  # below poverty line
    pov_universe = "B17001_001",  # poverty universe
    bach         = "B15003_022",  # bachelor's degree
    masters      = "B15003_023",  # master's degree
    professional = "B15003_024",  # professional degree
    doctorate    = "B15003_025",  # doctorate degree
    edu_universe = "B15003_001"   # education universe (25+)
  ),
  state  = "CO",
  year   = 2020,
  survey = "acs5"
)

# ============================================================
# STEP 8 — Reshape and join ACS to spatial data
# Walker Ch. 3.2 — pivot_wider() and left_join()
# Walker p.34 — Hispanic is ethnicity not race (separate table)
# ============================================================
denver_acs_wide <- denver_acs %>%
  select(GEOID, variable, estimate) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = variable, values_from = estimate)

denver_full <- denver_density %>%
  left_join(denver_acs_wide, by = "GEOID") %>%
  mutate(
    pct_white   = white    / total_pop   * 100,
    pct_black   = black    / total_pop   * 100,
    pct_hisp    = hispanic / total_pop   * 100,
    pct_poverty = below_pov / pov_universe * 100,
    pct_college = (bach + masters + professional + doctorate) /
                   edu_universe * 100
  )

denver_full_plot <- denver_full %>% st_transform(4326)

# ============================================================
# STEP 9 — Summary table by landscape
# Walker Ch. 3.3.1 — group_by() and summarize()
# ============================================================
landscape_summary <- denver_full %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(landscape) %>%
  summarize(
    total_pop   = sum(pop_total,   na.rm = TRUE),
    med_income  = median(med_income, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_white   = weighted.mean(pct_white,   pop_total,    na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_black   = weighted.mean(pct_black,   pop_total,    na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_hisp    = weighted.mean(pct_hisp,    pop_total,    na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_poverty = weighted.mean(pct_poverty, pov_universe, na.rm = TRUE),
    pct_college = weighted.mean(pct_college, edu_universe, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

print(landscape_summary, width = Inf)

# ============================================================
# STEP 10 — Income bar chart
# Walker Ch. 4.1 — ggplot2 geom_col()
# Walker Ch. 4.2.3 — ggsave()
# ============================================================
income_chart <- ggplot(
  landscape_summary,
  aes(x = landscape, y = med_income, fill = landscape)
) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = dollar(med_income)), vjust = -0.4, size = 3.5) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = dollar_format(),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.1))
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Urban High-Density"    = "#b2182b",
    "Urban Low-Density"     = "#ef8a62",
    "Suburban High-Density" = "#9ecae1",
    "Suburban Low-Density"  = "#d1e5f0",
    "Exurban"               = "#2166ac"
  )) +
  labs(
    title    = "Median Household Income by Landscape — Denver MSA",
    subtitle = "2016–2020 ACS 5-Year Estimates",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Median Household Income",
    caption  = "Source: U.S. Census Bureau ACS"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))

income_chart

# ============================================================
# STEP 11 — Poverty rate choropleth map
# Walker Ch. 6 — tmap/ggplot2 choropleth mapping
# ============================================================
denver_poverty_plot <- denver_full %>%
  st_transform(4326) %>%
  select(GEOID, pct_poverty, geometry) %>%
  filter(!is.na(pct_poverty)) %>%
  mutate(
    poverty_cat = cut(
      pct_poverty,
      breaks = c(0, 2.745, 5.189, 8.247, 12.802, 77.935),
      labels = c(
        "Very Low (0–2.7%)",
        "Low (2.7–5.2%)",
        "Moderate (5.2–8.2%)",
        "High (8.2–12.8%)",
        "Very High (12.8–77.9%)"
      ),
      include.lowest = TRUE
    )
  )

poverty_map <- ggplot(denver_poverty_plot) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = poverty_cat), color = "white", lwd = 0.1) +
  geom_sf(data = denver_msa_plot, fill = NA,
          color = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Very Low (0–2.7%)"      = "#fee5d9",
      "Low (2.7–5.2%)"         = "#fcae91",
      "Moderate (5.2–8.2%)"    = "#fb6a4a",
      "High (8.2–12.8%)"       = "#cb181d",
      "Very High (12.8–77.9%)" = "#67000d"
    ),
    name     = "% Below Poverty Line",
    na.value = "grey80"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Poverty Rate by Tract —\nDenver MSA (2020 ACS)",
    subtitle = "Quantile classification · 2016–2020 ACS 5-Year Estimates",
    caption  = "Source: U.S. Census Bureau ACS"
  ) +
  theme_void(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13,
                                   margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey40",
                                   margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.caption    = element_text(size = 8,  color = "grey50"),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title    = element_text(size = 9,  face = "bold"),
    legend.text     = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.margin     = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

poverty_map

# ============================================================
# STEP 12 — Racial composition bar chart
# Walker Ch. 4 — ggplot2 grouped bar chart
# Walker p.34 — Hispanic as separate ethnic category
# ============================================================
race_summary <- denver_full %>%
  st_drop_geometry() %>%
  group_by(landscape) %>%
  summarize(
    White    = weighted.mean(pct_white, pop_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    Black    = weighted.mean(pct_black, pop_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    Hispanic = weighted.mean(pct_hisp,  pop_total, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) %>%
  pivot_longer(
    cols      = c(White, Black, Hispanic),
    names_to  = "group",
    values_to = "pct"
  ) %>%
  mutate(group = factor(group, levels = c("White", "Hispanic", "Black")))

race_chart <- ggplot(
  race_summary,
  aes(x = landscape, y = pct, fill = group)
) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.7),
    vjust    = -0.4,
    size     = 2.8
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "White"    = "#2166ac",
      "Black"    = "#b2182b",
      "Hispanic" = "#f4a582"
    ),
    name = "Racial group"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = function(x) paste0(round(x), "%"),
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Racial Composition by Landscape — Denver MSA",
    subtitle = "Population-weighted averages · 2016–2020 ACS 5-Year Estimates",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Share of population (%)",
    caption  = "Source: U.S. Census Bureau ACS"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13,
                                    margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey40",
                                    margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.caption     = element_text(size = 8,  color = "grey50"),
    axis.text.x      = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1),
    legend.position  = "right",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
  )

race_chart

# ============================================================
# STEP 13 — Hispanic population share map
# Walker Ch. 6 — choropleth mapping
# ============================================================
denver_hisp_plot <- denver_full %>%
  st_transform(4326) %>%
  select(GEOID, pct_hisp, geometry) %>%
  filter(!is.na(pct_hisp)) %>%
  mutate(
    hisp_cat = cut(
      pct_hisp,
      breaks = c(0, 10, 20, 35, 50, 100),
      labels = c(
        "Very Low (0–10%)",
        "Low (10–20%)",
        "Moderate (20–35%)",
        "High (35–50%)",
        "Very High (50–100%)"
      ),
      include.lowest = TRUE
    )
  )

hisp_map <- ggplot(denver_hisp_plot) +
  geom_sf(aes(fill = hisp_cat), color = "white", lwd = 0.1) +
  geom_sf(data = denver_msa_plot, fill = NA,
          color = "black", lwd = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c(
      "Very Low (0–10%)"    = "#f7f4f9",
      "Low (10–20%)"        = "#d4b9da",
      "Moderate (20–35%)"   = "#c994c7",
      "High (35–50%)"       = "#df65b0",
      "Very High (50–100%)" = "#91003f"
    ),
    name     = "% Hispanic",
    na.value = "grey80"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Hispanic Population Share by Tract —\nDenver MSA",
    subtitle = "2016–2020 ACS 5-Year Estimates",
    caption  = "Source: U.S. Census Bureau ACS"
  ) +
  theme_void(base_size = 11) +
  theme(
    plot.title      = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13,
                                   margin = margin(b = 4)),
    plot.subtitle   = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey40",
                                   margin = margin(b = 8)),
    plot.caption    = element_text(size = 8,  color = "grey50"),
    legend.position = "right",
    legend.title    = element_text(size = 9,  face = "bold"),
    legend.text     = element_text(size = 8),
    plot.margin     = margin(10, 10, 10, 10)
  )

hisp_map

# ============================================================
# STEP 14 — Population pyramids
# Walker Ch. 4.5 p.79-82 — population pyramids
# B01001 = Sex by Age table
# Males flipped negative per Walker p.81
# ============================================================
male_vars_list   <- paste0("B01001_0", str_pad(3:25,  2, pad = "0"))
female_vars_list <- paste0("B01001_0", str_pad(27:49, 2, pad = "0"))

age_sex <- get_acs(
  geography = "tract",
  variables = c(male_vars_list, female_vars_list),
  state     = "CO",
  year      = 2020,
  survey    = "acs5"
)

male_vars   <- paste0("B01001_0", str_pad(3:25,  2, pad = "0"))
female_vars <- paste0("B01001_0", str_pad(27:49, 2, pad = "0"))

age_labels <- c(
  "Under 5", "5–9", "10–14", "15–17", "18–19",
  "20", "21", "22–24", "25–29", "30–34",
  "35–39", "40–44", "45–49", "50–54", "55–59",
  "60–61", "62–64", "65–66", "67–69", "70–74",
  "75–79", "80–84", "85+"
)

urban_ids <- denver_density %>%
  filter(str_detect(landscape, "Urban")) %>%
  pull(GEOID)

suburban_ids <- denver_density %>%
  filter(str_detect(landscape, "Suburban")) %>%
  pull(GEOID)

exurban_ids <- denver_density %>%
  filter(landscape == "Exurban") %>%
  pull(GEOID)

make_pyramid_data <- function(geoids) {
  age_sex %>%
    filter(GEOID %in% geoids) %>%
    mutate(sex = case_when(
      variable %in% male_vars   ~ "Male",
      variable %in% female_vars ~ "Female",
      TRUE                      ~ NA_character_
    )) %>%
    filter(!is.na(sex)) %>%
    group_by(variable, sex) %>%
    summarize(pop = sum(estimate, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop") %>%
    group_by(sex) %>%
    mutate(age_group = age_labels) %>%
    ungroup() %>%
    mutate(
      age_group = factor(age_group, levels = age_labels),
      pop       = if_else(sex == "Male", -pop, pop)
    )
}

make_pyramid_plot <- function(data, title_text) {
  max_val <- max(abs(data$pop), na.rm = TRUE)
  ggplot(data, aes(x = pop, y = age_group, fill = sex)) +
    geom_col(width = 0.85, alpha = 0.85) +
    scale_x_continuous(
      labels = ~ scales::number(abs(.x),
                                scale    = 1e-3,
                                suffix   = "k",
                                accuracy = 1),
      limits = c(-max_val, max_val),
      breaks = pretty(c(-max_val, max_val), n = 6)
    ) +
    scale_fill_manual(
      values = c(Female = "#b2182b", Male = "#2166ac")
    ) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "white", lwd = 0.8) +
    labs(
      title    = title_text,
      subtitle = "Denver MSA · 2016–2020 ACS",
      x        = "Population",
      y        = "Age Group",
      fill     = "",
      caption  = "Source: U.S. Census Bureau ACS B01001"
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +
    theme(
      plot.title       = element_text(face = "bold", size = 13,
                                      margin = margin(b = 4)),
      plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 10, color = "grey40",
                                      margin = margin(b = 8)),
      plot.caption     = element_text(size = 8,  color = "grey50"),
      legend.position  = "bottom",
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank()
    )
}

urban_pyramid_data    <- make_pyramid_data(urban_ids)
suburban_pyramid_data <- make_pyramid_data(suburban_ids)
exurban_pyramid_data  <- make_pyramid_data(exurban_ids)

pyramid_urban <- make_pyramid_plot(
  urban_pyramid_data,
  "Population Pyramid — Urban Tracts\nDenver MSA"
)

pyramid_suburban <- make_pyramid_plot(
  suburban_pyramid_data,
  "Population Pyramid — Suburban Tracts\nDenver MSA"
)

pyramid_exurban <- make_pyramid_plot(
  exurban_pyramid_data,
  "Population Pyramid — Exurban Tracts\nDenver MSA"
)

pyramid_urban
pyramid_suburban
pyramid_exurban



# ============================================================
# STEP 15 — Confirm all output files saved
# ============================================================
list.files(pattern = "\\.png$")
# (keep all your existing code here, just no ggsave lines needed)
```

## Overview

This study examines demographic change and spatial inequality across the Denver-Aurora Metropolitan Statistical Area. Using data from the 2020 Decennial Census and the 2016-2020 American Community Survey (ACS), census tracts are classified into urban, suburban, and exurban landscape types based on population density. This classification is then used to examine how income, poverty, racial composition, and age structure vary across this landscape, addressing the question: how do these dimensions of inequality differ across the urban–suburban–exurban continuum, and what do these patterns reveal about metropolitan growth in Denver?
Census tracts are classified using population density thresholds adapted from Hanberry (2022), which distinguishes urban, suburban, and exurban landscapes based on persons per square kilometer. This framework provides a systematic basis for comparing demographic patterns across landscape types and allows for the analysis of spatial variation that may be obscured when relying solely on administrative boundaries.




## Landscape Classification

Urban high-density tracts concentrate in the city core while suburban 
and exurban tracts extend toward the periphery.

```{r}
landscape_map
```

## Income

Median household income rises moving from urban to suburban and exurban 
landscapes, reflecting uneven distribution of economic gains across the metro.

```{r}
income_chart
```

## Poverty

Poverty concentrates heavily in urban core tracts, particularly in 
north and west Denver.

```{r}
poverty_map
```

## Racial Composition

Hispanic residents concentrate in urban and inner suburban tracts while 
suburban and exurban landscapes remain predominantly white.

```{r}
race_chart
hisp_map
```

## Age Structure

Urban tracts skew younger, suburban tracts reflect family households, 
and exurban tracts show an older age profile.

```{r}
pyramid_urban
pyramid_suburban
pyramid_exurban
```

## What the Data Reveal

Taken together, these patterns tell a consistent story about uneven 
development in the Denver metro area. Suburban and exurban tracts 
concentrate wealth, older family households, and predominantly white 
populations, while urban core tracts bear the highest poverty rates 
and are home to the metro's Hispanic and Black residents. The density 
gradient is not simply a technical classification — it maps onto deeper 
inequalities of race and income that shape who benefits from Denver's 
growth and who remains on the margins.
